Media Server Control API for the Java Platform

Provides a java application with a set of media resources capabilities and media composition operations Package Specification The Media Server Control API provides media services for IP calls.

See:
          Description

Packages
javax.media.mscontrol Provides a java Application Server with a Media Server Control API.
javax.media.mscontrol.join An application joins media objects to establish media streams between them.
javax.media.mscontrol.mediagroup A MediaGroup is mostly a player/recorder.
javax.media.mscontrol.mediagroup.http Mediagroup parameters specialized for HTTP file play and record
javax.media.mscontrol.mediagroup.signals provides resources for detecting and generating signals: DTMF, speech, .... for any type of media
javax.media.mscontrol.mixer A MediaMixer has the capacity to combine the media streams coming from multiple sources, into a single media stream.
javax.media.mscontrol.networkconnection NetworkConnections manage a network termination point, using RTP for an IP network.
javax.media.mscontrol.resource This package provides the base definitions for managing media processing resources.
javax.media.mscontrol.resource.common Manage resources that can be inserted in different ResourceContainers like MediaGroups, NetworkConnections or MixerAdapters.
javax.media.mscontrol.resource.video Capabilites to handle video resources, layout and rendering.
javax.media.mscontrol.spi This package provides classes and interfaces defining a Driver service provider interface (SPI).
javax.media.mscontrol.vxml Capabilities to control VoiceXML dialogs that are delegated to the Media Server.

 

Provides a java application with a set of media resources capabilities and media composition operations

Package Specification

The Media Server Control API provides media services for IP calls.

  • An application gets a group of media resources (a MediaGroup) from the media server. Operations on a MediaGroup include :

    • record() operation

    • play(URI[])

    • receiveSignals()

    Many kind of asynchronous MediaEvent can be caught by Listeners:

    • RecorderEvent

    • PlayerEvent

    • SignalDetectorEvent


  • A NetworkConnection drives resources like RTP ports or echo cancellers.
    Network ports are configured and driven by SDP session descriptions exchanged over the signalling path.
    A SdpPortManager Resource is capable to connect the media ports as required by the signaling, in the context of an incoming call, an outgoing call, or a media change.
    The asynchronous SdpPortManagerEvents are caught by an application listener set by addListener().

  • MediaGroup and NetworkConnection are ResourceContainers, and can be obtained with MediaSession.createMediaGroup(Configuration aConfig) and MediaSession.createNetworkConnection(Configuration aConfig).

  • An MediaGroup can be joined to a NetworkConnection, and in that case the media stream played by the MediaGroup will be heard/seen by the remote caller, and the media stream coming from the remote caller can be recorded by the MediaGroup; the DTMF keys pressed by the remote caller will be caught by a receiveSignals running on the MediaGroup.

  • Two remote parties can be bridged by joining their two NetworkConnections.

  • Conference, as other multiparty applications, requires to use a MediaMixer. A MediaMixer has the capacity to mix multiple media streams.

  • Higher levels of media control are also available, with JoinableDialogs. A JoinableDialog exposes high-level abstractions, like the VxmlDialog object, in package javax.media.mscontrol.vxml.

  • All operations can be fine-tuned with Parameters classes, providing values for each Parameter applicable to the operation.

Related Documentation

  • UML diagram of the main classes

  • UML diagram of most classes

  • how an application specifies required resources: For example, to configure a MediaGroup: MediaGroup.PLAYER

  • how to apply the SDP (received from signalling) to a NetworkConnection: SdpPortManager

  • how to use the resources in a MediaGroup as well as links to the various features below :

  • recording sound files, see Recorder

  • playing back such files, see Player

  • producing DTMFs, see SignalGenerator

  • converting text-to-speech, see Player

  • detecting/recognizing signals in a stream, like DTMF signals, see SignalDetector

  • performing speech-recognition, see SignalDetector

  • having talks / chats / conferencing with multiple call parties, see MediaMixer

  • with a MediaEventListener, catch asynchronous events, like PlayerEvent, as well as RecorderEvent, SignalDetectorEvent and SignalGeneratorEvent
